questions.question: What is the strongest reason to prefer an event interface over a synchronous API?
options: The caller needs an immediate answer before returning to the user.
Many downstream consumers need the same state change and can process it asynchronously.
The system should avoid all schema management work.
The service has only one internal consumer and no fanout.
questions.explanation: Events are strongest when multiple consumers react asynchronously to the same state change.

questions.question: Why do many public APIs stay on HTTP even when internal services use gRPC?
options: HTTP is easier for diverse clients, proxies, and debugging tools to support.
gRPC cannot express typed request and response contracts.
HTTP guarantees lower latency than every RPC framework.
Public APIs never need versioning or compatibility plans.
questions.explanation: Public APIs optimize for compatibility and ecosystem reach, not just internal efficiency.

questions.question: Which boundary is the best fit for a payment authorization result that must be known before checkout completes?
options: A replayable event consumed whenever downstream systems are ready.
A synchronous interface such as HTTP or gRPC on the critical path.
A batch export written once per hour.
A best-effort webhook sent to every dependent service.
questions.explanation: Checkout cannot complete safely without a direct answer, so the commit path should stay synchronous.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off when replacing a chain of synchronous calls with events for side effects?
options: You gain decoupling and replay, but accept eventual consistency and more debugging complexity.
You eliminate schema evolution because events never change shape.
You guarantee lower end-to-end latency for the user-facing path.
You remove the need for observability on downstream consumers.
questions.explanation: Events improve decoupling, but they also make timing and failure analysis more distributed.

questions.question: Which signal best indicates that an event boundary is becoming unhealthy?
options: Event lag and redelivery counts climbing for critical consumers.
The public API documentation getting longer than expected.
A decrease in static type generation time.
The number of HTTP headers used by a browser client.
questions.explanation: Lag and redelivery expose delivery pressure, consumer slowness, and replay churn.

questions.question: When would gRPC usually be a stronger choice than HTTP between services?
options: When the services are externally consumed by unknown clients over many environments.
When a low-latency internal boundary benefits from typed contracts and both sides are controlled together.
When the interaction is really a many-consumer domain event.
When the system wants to avoid release coordination entirely.
questions.explanation: gRPC shines most on controlled, internal service-to-service boundaries.

questions.question: What design mistake often appears when engineers say "everything is an API"?
options: They may hide whether the boundary is a command, query, or event and miss the real failure model.
They automatically select the correct interface for every workload.
They eliminate any need for ownership boundaries.
They reduce operational complexity across all teams.
questions.explanation: Calling everything an API can blur important differences in timing, ownership, and retries.

questions.question: If a single state change must trigger analytics, email, and fulfillment, what is the best default design starting point?
options: Make the user request wait on all three downstream systems synchronously.
Expose a public gRPC endpoint directly to each downstream team.
Emit a durable event after the primary transaction commits and let consumers react independently.
Poll the database every second from each consumer.
questions.explanation: A durable event keeps the primary path smaller while supporting fanout and replay.
